The $MU / Anthropic deal headlines the obvious name… but the second-order effects are where the tape gets interesting. 
 
Because when you zoom out, it’s not just Micron. 
 
$LRCX - the equipment behind HBM manufacturing 
$ENTG - materials layer inside advanced chips 
$AMKR - packaging flow scaling with output 
$VRT - cooling + infrastructure inside AI data centers 
 
That’s the part most people miss - AI demand doesn’t show up in one ticker, it leaks across the entire supply chain. 
 
And today, ironically, several of these names are down ~8–10% even with “positive AI news” in the background. 
 
That disconnect between narrative and price is where things usually get re-rated… one way or the other. 
 
Watching whether this is just rotation noise or the start of a broader repricing across the AI infrastructure stack.

BoA: AI is set to compress into five years what took the chip industry half a century to achieve - identifies 5 key forces they believe will drive the next $1T in semiconductor sales 
 
"The chip industry took ~50 years to generate its first $1T in sales. We expect AI to help add another $1T in just the next 5 years." 
 
The bank’s 5 themes are: 
1) AI data center systems, whose TAM it sees growing to roughly $1.7T by 2030 from $273B in 2025 
2) Memory strength & durability underpinned by long-term supply agreements 
3) Semiconductor capital equip't & reshoring benefiting from rising chip complexity 
4) Analog chips gaining from surging AI power requirements 
5) Agentic CPU demand, represents a $170B server opportunity across x86 & ARM architectures. 
 
Alongside the thematic update, BofA raised its total semiconductor industry forecast to $2.7T by 2030, implying a +28% CAGR from 2025, up from a prior est of $2.3T. The analysts see memory as the dominant, w/ BoA projecting nearly +300% y/y growth in 2026 alone. 
 
The bank also materially lifted its wafer fab equipment (WFE) f/casts, now seeing spending reach $250B by 2028, up from a prior est of $203B & potentially hitting $292B by 2030

Applied Materials shares rose after UBS upgraded the stock to Buy from Neutral and raised its price target to $285, arguing the company is poised to outperform the fast-growing wafer fabrication equipment market over the next two years. UBS highlighted strong exposure to memory and logic, especially DRAM, which it says is entering a demand “super-cycle” that investors are underestimating. The firm expects the WFE market to grow more than 20% in 2026 to $136.5 billion, potentially reaching $145 billion in 2027, with memory spending driving the gains. 
Despite AMAT’s 42% rise this year, the stock has trailed peers, but UBS says the underperformance doesn’t reflect its fundamentals. The firm also projects 11% growth in China’s WFE demand next year—contrary to broader Wall Street expectations—representing potential upside. Most analysts remain bullish, and UBS’s new target is among the highest on the Street.

KeyBanc analysts downgraded Lam Research to Sector Weight while turning more positive on Applied Materials, citing that Lam’s recent rally has outpaced its near-term earnings potential, whereas Applied offers a cheaper entry into the same favorable sector trends. 
Lam’s upgrade had been driven by optimism around memory recovery and AI-related spending, but KeyBanc noted stretched valuations, with Lam trading well above historical averages. Analysts highlighted that even assuming 10% above current consensus estimates, Lam would trade at 23x and 20x projected FY27-28 earnings versus its typical high-teen P/E. 
Applied Materials, in contrast, has lagged peers this year due to investor concerns over exposure to mature chip manufacturing in China and uneven demand at the technology frontier. KeyBanc said these issues are largely priced in, making AMAT more attractive at a reasonable valuation, raising its price target to $220.

United States President Donald Trump told the media at a White House dinner with technology executives that the US will soon impose tariffs on foreign semiconductor companies that do not invest domestically.  
 
"I have discussed it with people here," Trump said. "We will be putting a very substantial tariff very shortly," he added. Trump said the measure would target companies that manufacture chips abroad without plans to build facilities in the United States. He added that firms establishing or planning US operations would be exempt.

Computer hardware and storage stocks rallied Thursday, led by SanDisk, which jumped 13% on expectations of gains from new export regulations. Western Digital rose 5%, while Seagate Technology, Micron, and Lam Research each advanced about 3%. 
Analysts highlighted that restrictions on memory chip production in China could disrupt supply and lift flash memory prices. Lynx Equity’s KC Rajkumar called SanDisk and Micron the “clear winners,” noting SanDisk’s focused flash-storage business model gives it a particular edge. Lam Research was also seen as well-positioned to benefit. 
The export rules are expected to constrain output at Samsung and SK Hynix’s China-based NAND plants, tightening supply and creating favorable pricing conditions for competitors with operations outside China.
